region_of_origin: Philippines / Singapore (popularised in Singapore Chinese-Filipino restaurant context)
food_subgroup: Poultry dishes — deep-fried chicken with savoury sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Marmite Chicken. FOODON_00002725 (chicken food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Marmite-glazed fried chicken.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps specifically to Marmite Chicken as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Marmite Chicken is approximately 300 g, comprising several pieces of deep-fried chicken coated in a thick, glossy Marmite-based sauce, often served with steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Fried chicken with sauce — nutrient profile estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried chicken (coated, with skin) combined with Marmite yeast extract contribution
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy; Marmite product nutritional label (Unilever)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Marmite Chicken exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken (coated, skin on, USDA FDC NDB 05062) as the base, adjusted for Marmite glaze contribution (high sodium, moderate sugar, negligible fat addition). Marmite yeast extract nutritional data sourced from Unilever product label. Composite estimate reflects a typical restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed protein-fat-carbohydrate dishes — analogous estimate based on fried chicken with coating and sweet-savoury sauce; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Marmite Chicken. GI estimated at 45 (low category) based on analogous fried chicken with sweet-savoury coating dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The moderate carbohydrate content (cornstarch coating, sugar in sauce) combined with high protein and fat content suppresses glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (45 × 10.8 × 3.0) / 100 = 14.6 g for a 300 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from Marmite yeast extract, soy sauce, and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Marmite Chicken due to its high sodium content of approximately 620 mg per 100 g (approximately 1860 mg per standard 300 g serving), which represents over 80% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load with added sugars in Marmite glaze; low GI mitigates acute glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Marmite Chicken in controlled portions; while the estimated GI of 45 is in the low range, the glycemic load per serving is approximately 14.6 g and the added sugar content from the Marmite glaze warrants monitoring of total carbohydrate intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium, moderate saturated fat from deep-frying, and cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Marmite Chicken due to its combination of high sodium (approximately 1860 mg per serving), moderate saturated fat (3.8 g per 100 g), and cholesterol (72 mg per 100 g), all of which may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Marmite Chicken is a popular savoury dish found in Singapore and the Philippines, consisting of deep-fried chicken pieces — typically bone-in thighs, drumsticks, or wings — that are coated in a thick, glossy, umami-rich sauce made from Marmite yeast extract, soy sauce, oyster sauce, butter, garlic, and sugar. The chicken is first marinated, then deep-fried until crispy, and finally wok-tossed in the Marmite-based sauce to achieve a caramelised, sticky glaze. The flavour profile is intensely savoury with a slight sweetness and a distinctive yeasty depth from the Marmite. The texture contrasts a crispy exterior with juicy, tender chicken meat. Marmite Chicken is commonly served as a main dish alongside steamed white rice and is a staple in Chinese-Filipino restaurants and zi char (Singapore-style Chinese cook-to-order) establishments. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-to-high calorie dish at approximately 242 kcal per 100 g, with a good protein content of 18.5 g per 100 g from the chicken. It is notably high in sodium (approximately 620 mg per 100 g) due to the Marmite, soy sauce, and oyster sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish contains gluten from Marmite (which is derived from barley/wheat) and soy allergens. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal-certified food without specific preparation adjustments.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Marmite Chicken?
What does Marmite Chicken taste like?
Is Marmite Chicken Filipino or Singaporean?
How much sodium is in Marmite Chicken?
What is the glycemic index of Marmite Chicken?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Marmite Chicken was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken (coated, skin on) as the closest base, adjusted for the Marmite glaze contribution. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is transparent, the base food is well-characterised, and the Marmite contribution is quantifiable from product label data.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no exact Marmite Chicken entry exists in any approved primary database. Values should be validated against laboratory analysis or a future HPB Singapore Foodo entry if one becomes available. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy fried chicken source entry and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy fried chicken source entry for this preparation. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source estimate for this dish. 5. Halal status is marked false because Marmite (Unilever) is not universally certified halal in all markets; consumers should verify local product certification. 6. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on fried chicken with sweet-savoury coating from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008; no direct GI measurement for Marmite Chicken was found. 7. The cuisine tag 'Filipino' is preserved from the input; however, this dish is also strongly associated with Singapore zi char cuisine and Chinese-Filipino restaurant culture in Singapore. 8. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1 through vitamin_b12, folate, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy NDB 05062 (chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter) and adjusted proportionally for sauce contribution.
